# cat-task-manager

A TUI for managing small daily recurring tasks. Keyboard-operated. Written in Rust.

*Note: Much of the following text was generated by AI and may be difficult to read; I plan to manually revise it later.*

This application is not a general-purpose TODO app. Rather than handling projects, deadlines, priorities, tags, searching, or complex dependencies, it prioritizes seamlessly progressing through "today's fixed sequence of tasks."

## Philosophy

Daily tasks tend not to continue if their management becomes too elaborate.
What's needed is to immediately see the task that needs attention, to record the start time, to move to the next task upon completion, and for on-hand operations to be lightweight.

Therefore, cat-task-manager does not have features that encourage adding too many tasks.
Tasks are not hierarchical. They don't have parallel dependencies. They don't have per-task notes or attributes.
To maintain the daily flow, it strongly focuses solely on progressing through tasks sequentially.

`tasks.txt` is the single source of truth for tasks and their current-day in-progress status. Tasks are not written in the config.
It does not maintain a separate status directory.
Prioritizing the ETC (Easy To Change) principle, it focuses on making tasks frequently maintainable by consolidating what the user interacts with into a single text file.

```text
- [ ] 朝のルーティン
- [ ] メール確認
- [ ] コードレビュー
```

With this format, you don't need to duplicate table or field names when adding tasks.
The top-to-bottom order directly becomes the execution order, and if you want to change the order, you just move the lines.

## Background

"Everybody has their own habits," and a task management app for daily life.

In an era where "vibecoding" has become possible,
there's no longer a need to adapt one's habits to a task management app;
it's faster to "vibecode" a task management app that fits one's own habits.

That's why I vibecoded it.
It is not intended for others to use.
I will frequently make destructive changes without notice.
That said, I hope that by sharing this, someone might gain some kind of hint or inspiration.

## Implementation Philosophy

Task definitions and current status are consolidated in `tasks.txt`.
Normal non-empty lines are task definitions, and the status is read as JSON at the end of each task line.
The end-of-line JSON is the area written by the application.

```text
- [x] 朝のルーティン {"date":"2026-05-19","state":"done","started_at":"2026-05-19T09:00:00+09:00","completed_at":"2026-05-19T09:05:00+09:00"}
- [ ] メール確認 {"date":"2026-05-19","state":"in_progress","started_at":"2026-05-19T09:12:00+09:00"}
- [ ] コードレビュー {"date":"2026-05-19","state":"not_started"}
```

This is a compromise to avoid duplicating state into separate files.
Even if you correct a typo in a task name, the state will not disappear if you preserve the end-of-line JSON for that line.
If lines are added, deleted, or reordered, the state will move along with the same line.
Tasks marked `- [x]` without end-of-line JSON will be normalized into end-of-line JSON with the detected time as both start and completion times.
If the end-of-line JSON format is corrupted, it will be treated as an error on startup or reload.

Only past records are written out to `records` by date.
This is not for current-day operations but rather the result after the day ends.
To preserve `tasks.txt` as the SSoT, it is not used for current-day restoration.

The display is designed to focus on the next task, while also allowing the full list to be checked only when necessary.
Immediately after startup, it displays one line; `v` toggles between one-line, incomplete, and full views.
In incomplete view, completed items are removed; in full view, completed items are included.
For completed items, the working time is displayed based on start and completion times. Operable items are limited to not-started, in-progress, on-hold, and deferred tasks.

States are kept few: not started, in progress, done, on hold, deferred, and timed out.
"On hold" blocks the next task, while "deferred" is used to proceed to the next task without blocking.
When a task is on hold in the single-line view of an individual tab, it displays a status explanation to facilitate moving to other tabs.
When the date changes, uncompleted tasks are treated as timed out in that day's record.
This is a compromise not to complicate deadlines or scheduling, but simply to record that "it wasn't finished that day."

Operations are centered around keybindings. Starting and completing are advanced with the same key; holding (to other tabs) and resuming are also toggled with the same key.
Deferring is also toggled on/off with key operations.
`tasks.txt` can also be opened with a key operation, and reloaded upon closing.
Entrusting task editing to a familiar editor is faster than creating forms within the app, and it's easier to inspect content in case of failure.

## Configuration Structure

`config.toml` only holds settings for editor candidates, keybindings, and startup Git snapshots. It does not contain tasks.

```toml
editors = ["fresh", "zed", "nvim", "code"]

[startup_git]
auto_commit_and_push = false

[keybindings]
j = "next"
down = "next"
k = "previous"
up = "previous"
enter = "advance"
space = "advance"
p = "hold"
d = "defer"
q = "quit"
e = "edit"
l = "next_tab"
right = "next_tab"
h = "previous_tab"
left = "previous_tab"
v = "toggle_view"
"?" = "help"
```

Only when `startup_git.auto_commit_and_push = true`, `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager` will be git committed and pushed once a day on startup.

The storage location is consolidated under Windows' `AppData Local`.

```text
%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager\config.toml
%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager\tasks.txt
%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager\records\YYYY-MM-DD.toml
```

`tasks.txt` is the SSoT that both the user and the app read from and write to. `config.toml` is for operational environment settings. `records` is treated as the output destination for past results.

## Deliberate Choice

This app is designed not as a "place to manage tasks," but as a "place to progress through a fixed daily routine."

For flexible TODO management, searching, tagging, and deadline management, use other tools.
cat-task-manager starts the same way every day, progresses from top to bottom, and records anything not completed as that day's result.
Preserving that simplicity is a top implementation priority.
